meta.description: Act 4 — The War. The city erupts into full faction warfare. District battles, NPC deaths, and 15 endings based on player choices across Acts 1-3.

act4_001.title: The Morning After
text: You wake to the sound of something you have never heard in the Lower District: silence. No drone patrols. No vendor calls. No hum of the stack relay towers. The absence of noise is so total it feels like pressure against your eardrums.
Then the first explosion shakes the building to its foundation and the silence dies screaming.

Dust rains from the ceiling. The window cracks in a spiderweb pattern, and through it you see a column of black smoke rising from the direction of Midcity. A second explosion follows, closer, and the power cuts out. Emergency strips along the floor flicker red, casting everything in the color of blood.
Whatever you did at the Meridian Core, whatever choice you made in that server room, the consequences have arrived. And they are measured in fire.

text.text: The kill code worked. Every cortical stack in the city crashed simultaneously at 3:47 AM. Millions of backed-up consciousnesses — erased. The rich, the powerful, the immortal — they are mortal again. And some of them, the ones mid-transfer, the ones running on borrowed bodies — they simply stopped. Hundreds of Highcity elites dropped dead in their penthouse beds. The stack network is gone. You destroyed it. And now the city is tearing itself apart.

The modified code did what you designed it to do. LATENCY is disabled — the hidden backdoor that let Meridian rewrite personalities, erase memories, control anyone with a stack. But the stacks themselves still function. People can still back up, still transfer. What they cannot do is be silently puppeted. When the city woke up and realized what Meridian had been doing to them, realized their thoughts had not always been their own — the rage was immediate and absolute.

You walked away from the console. Left the kill code unexecuted, the modification unfinished. But knowledge is its own weapon. The data you pulled from the Meridian Core spread through the underground networks like a virus. Proof of LATENCY. Proof that Meridian could rewrite anyone with a stack. The revolution you refused to start began anyway — just slower, messier, and without a clean surgical strike to give it shape.

You chose the winning side. That is what you told yourself when you handed the kill code back to Director Voss and watched him smile like a father welcoming home a prodigal child. Meridian rewarded you: credits, a Highcity apartment, a stack upgrade that hums with clean power. But the underground found out. Every faction, every contact, every friend you ever made in the Lower District knows what you did. You are a traitor. And in this city, traitors burn.

choices.text: Look out the window to assess the damage
Check your datapad for messages

act4_002.title: A City on Fire
text: The view through the cracked window is apocalyptic. Three separate columns of smoke rise from different districts, black against a sky already grey with smog. In the direction of Midcity, an entire block is burning — you can see the orange glow pulsing between buildings like a living thing. Highcity's towers still stand, but their running lights are dark. Dead spires against a wounded sky.

Closer, in the streets below, people are running. Not the usual hurried shuffle of Lower District life but the flat sprint of genuine terror. A Meridian security transport lies overturned at the intersection, its armor cracked open. Bodies in corporate security uniforms are scattered around it. Someone has spray-painted LATENCY KILLS on the hull in red.

A formation of combat drones sweeps overhead, not the usual patrol models but military-grade units with visible weapons pods. They are heading toward the Undercity entrance. Behind them, a civilian transport with Iron Vanguard markings follows at street level, its cargo bed packed with armed figures.
The war has started. The only question left is which side of it you are standing on.

text.text: In the streets below, you see something that stops your breath. A woman in an expensive Highcity nightgown, barefoot, stumbling through the Lower District crowds with blood on her face. She is screaming. Behind her, two more Highcity residents in various states of undress, all of them looking around with the stunned incomprehension of people who have never set foot below the cloud line. Their stacks crashed. Their backup bodies failed. And now they are down here, mortal and terrified, surrounded by people who have every reason to hate them.

Your Meridian-issued datapad buzzes with a priority alert. Director Voss's face appears, calm as ever: 'Your former associates are making noise. We need you to help us quiet them. Report to Meridian Forward Command in Midcity. Your new access codes are attached.' You feel the weight of the choice you made pressing against the inside of your skull like a tumor.

choices.text: Head to the streets and find your contacts
Check your datapad first

act4_003.title: Messages from the Dead
text: Your datapad is flooded. Hundreds of messages, most from routing addresses you do not recognize, cascading in faster than you can read. The network is fragmenting — messages arriving out of order, some corrupted, some repeating. You filter for known contacts and the list that remains is short and desperate.
FROM MAKO: 'Shop is barricaded. Iron Vanguard took the east block. If you're alive get here. Bring weapons.' Timestamp: four hours ago.
FROM KIRA: 'Ghost Saints mobilizing at the old cathedral. Ash Collective hitting Meridian supply lines in Midcity. This is it. Choose a side or get buried in the crossfire.' Timestamp: two hours ago.
FROM ZERO: 'Running Undercity evacuation routes for civilians. Need bodies. Need help. People are dying down here and nobody cares because they're poor.' Timestamp: forty minutes ago.
FROM JADE: 'I have information about Meridian's endgame. Meet me at the Rust Garden. Come alone or don't come at all.' Timestamp: twelve minutes ago.
One final message sits at the bottom, from an address you do not recognize. It contains no text, just a set of coordinates in the Underground and a single word: COME.

text.text: None of them know you turned. Not yet. These messages were sent to the person they thought you were. The person who fought beside them. Every word is a knife made of trust and you are holding it by the blade.

A sixth message blinks in, flagged with a synth-only encryption header. 'Pixel is alive. Found her in sector 9 drainage. She is asking for you. Please.' The sender ID traces back to a synth collective you helped once. The message carries authentication codes that mark it as genuine.

choices.text: Go to Mako's shop — loyalty first
Head to Zero in the Undercity — civilians need help
Meet Jade at the Rust Garden — information wins wars
Follow the mysterious coordinates to the Underground
Report to Meridian Forward Command

act4_004.title: Mako's Last Stand
text: Mako's shop is a fortress. The old weapons dealer has spent decades preparing for exactly this kind of day, and it shows. Steel shutters reinforced with scavenged armor plating cover every window. The door opens only after three separate verification scans and a verbal passphrase that changes hourly.

Inside, Mako stands behind a counter covered in weapons. Not the usual trade stock — these are the pieces from the back room, the ones that were never for sale. Military-grade pulse rifles. Fragmentation charges. An ancient ballistic shotgun that looks like it was manufactured before the first stack was ever built. His metal arm gleams under the emergency lighting as he loads magazines with mechanical precision.

"Took you long enough," he says without looking up. "Iron Vanguard is pushing east. Ash Collective is hitting Meridian convoys in Midcity. Ghost Saints are doing what Ghost Saints do — disappearing and cutting throats. And down in the Undercity, people are drowning because some genius blew the water main to block the tunnels."
He finally looks at you. His one good eye is bloodshot. He has not slept.

"I'm not going to ask what you did at the Meridian Core. Don't care right now. What I care about is this: there are four districts and they are all burning. I can arm you. I can point you in a direction. But you have to pick a fight and commit to it, because there aren't enough of us to fight everywhere."

text.text: Mako pauses, and something complicated crosses his scarred face. 'The stacks are gone. You know that means my backup is gone too, right? Twenty years of memories I stored as insurance. Gone.' He picks up the shotgun and racks a round with his metal hand. 'So let's make sure I don't need them.'

A squad of orc fighters from the dockworker union are camped in the back room, their bulk making the space feel even smaller. Their leader, a scarred woman named Tusk, nods at you with grim recognition. 'Heard you were the one who got into Meridian Core. My people want to follow you. Give us a direction and we will break whatever is in the way.'

choices.text: "I'll fight for the Undercity. The civilians need protection."
"Midcity. That's where Meridian's forces are thickest."
"I'm going up. Highcity. Cut the head off the snake."
"The Underground. Something is happening down there."

act4_005.title: Zero's Evacuation
text: The Undercity entrance is chaos. Hundreds of people are trying to push their way down into the tunnels while hundreds more are trying to climb out. The two streams collide at the stairwell in a crush of bodies that smells like sweat and fear and something chemical — gas, maybe, drifting down from the street fighting above.

Zero is standing on a broken utility box, shouting over the crowd, trying to direct traffic with nothing but her voice and the force of her personality. She is small, dark-skinned, her hair shaved to stubble, and she looks like she has not stopped moving in days. A medic's satchel is slung across her chest and it is nearly empty.

"South tunnel is flooded past the third junction! North tunnel is clear to the service exit at Pier Nine! Families with children go north! Anyone who can fight stays here and helps me hold this entrance!"
She sees you and something in her face cracks — just for a second, relief bleeding through the composure before she walls it back up.

"Thank god. I need you. Meridian is sending sec-teams into the tunnels to flush people out. They're not distinguishing between fighters and civilians. I've got three hundred people in the south gallery with nowhere to go and the water is rising."

text.text: Your stonework instincts read the tunnel infrastructure at a glance. The flooding isn't natural — someone detonated charges along the water main to use the tunnels as a weapon. But the old drainage system runs deeper, pre-war construction that most people have forgotten. You could reroute the flood if you can reach the control valves in the lower levels.

Zero sees your medic kit and almost smiles. 'We've got forty wounded in the north gallery. Burns, crush injuries, two gunshot wounds. I ran out of synth-skin patches an hour ago and I'm using rags for bandages. Whatever you've got, it won't be enough. But it'll be more than nothing.'

choices.text: Help Zero hold the entrance against Meridian sec-teams
Go south to rescue the trapped civilians
"I'll come back. There's something I need to do first."

act4_006.title: The Rust Garden
text: The Rust Garden is a Midcity landmark — an abandoned rooftop greenhouse where the metal framework has corroded into shapes that almost look intentional, like sculpture. Dead plants still hang from their trellises, desiccated into brown architecture. In better times, fixers used it as a neutral meeting ground. Nothing is neutral anymore.

Jade is waiting at the center, sitting on a rusted bench with her legs crossed and a cigarette burning between her fingers. Jade runs information. No faction, no loyalty, no allegiance — just data, sold to the highest bidder. She is the most dangerous person you know who has never held a weapon.
"You came alone. Smart. Sit."
She does not wait for you to sit.

"Meridian has a contingency plan. They always have a contingency plan. Director Voss did not build an empire by assuming things would go well." She takes a drag. Her eyes are flat and hard. "The plan is called Protocol Ashfall. If the corporation loses control of the city, they burn it. Not metaphorically. They have thermobaric charges embedded in the Undercity support columns. Detonate them and the entire Underground collapses. Midcity sinks. Thousands dead. Maybe tens of thousands."

She holds up a data chip between two fingers. "This contains the detonation codes and the locations of every charge. I stole it from a Meridian executive who is now floating in the harbor. I'm giving it to you because you're the only person I've met in this city who might actually use it to save people instead of leverage."
"Or maybe I'm wrong about you. Guess we'll find out."

text.text: Your mind is already running calculations. Thermobaric charges in the support columns — that is not a contingency plan, that is a scorched earth protocol. If Voss triggers Ashfall, it will not just collapse the Underground. The structural damage will cascade upward. Midcity's foundations will fail within hours. Only Highcity, built on independent supports, would survive. Voss is willing to kill tens of thousands to keep his towers standing.

choices.text: Take the chip. Head to the Undercity to disarm the charges.
Take the chip. Go to Midcity to fight Meridian forces directly.
Take the chip. Storm Highcity and confront Voss.
"What's in it for you, Jade? Why help?"

act4_007.title: The Deep Signal
text: The coordinates lead you below the Undercity, below the service tunnels, below the ancient sewer systems, into rock that predates the city itself. Your light cuts a narrow path through darkness so complete it feels solid. The air down here tastes of minerals and something else — something that makes the back of your neck tingle like a static charge building before a lightning strike.

The tunnel opens into a cavern that should not exist. The walls are smooth, almost polished, and covered in markings that glow faint violet. Not paint. Not technology. Something older. The light they emit pulses in a rhythm that your hindbrain recognizes as a heartbeat even though your conscious mind rejects the idea.

At the center of the cavern, a rift. There is no other word for it. A vertical tear in the air itself, edges crackling with energy that is neither light nor electricity but something your eyes have no framework to process. Through it, you see nothing. Not darkness — the absence of the concept of seeing. A place where observation itself breaks down.

Ember is standing in front of it. The void-touched mystic who has been warning about this since you first met her in the Underground. She turns and her eyes are wrong — the irises replaced with the same violet glow as the wall markings.

"It heard you," she says, and her voice carries harmonics that a human throat should not produce. "When you accessed the Meridian Core, you touched the deep network. The network that runs beneath the stacks. The one Meridian built their technology on top of without understanding what it was."
The rift pulses. The heartbeat accelerates.
"It's waking up. And the war above is feeding it."

text.text: The void rift calls to you in a language you have always known but never spoken. Your shadow-blood sings in response, every cell in your body resonating with the energy pouring through the tear. You understand, with a clarity that terrifies you, that you could step through. That the other side is where your kind originally came from. That it wants you back.

Home. The word surfaces unbidden, rising from a place deeper than memory. The rift is a door and you have the key written into your DNA. The voidborn were not evolved — they were sent. Emissaries from the other side, given flesh to walk in a world of matter. The mission was forgotten across generations. But the door remembers.

choices.text: "How do we close it?"
Step closer to the rift
Leave. The war above matters more than whatever this is.

act4_008.title: Meridian Forward Command
text: The Meridian Forward Operating Base occupies what used to be a Midcity shopping complex. The storefronts are sandbagged, the atrium converted into a command center bristling with holographic tactical displays. Corporate security forces move with trained efficiency, checking weapons, loading transports, reviewing drone feeds. This is not a police action. This is a military operation.

Director Voss is not here in person — he would never risk himself this close to the fighting. But his face fills the main display, watching the battle unfold from the safety of his Highcity tower with the detached interest of a man observing a chess game.

Commander Rask meets you at the entrance. She is Meridian's field commander — ex-military, chrome jaw replacement, eyes that process threat assessments faster than most people blink. She looks at you the way a butcher looks at a tool.

"The asset from the Core operation. Good. You know the Lower District. You know the faction leaders. You know their hideouts, their escape routes, their supply caches." She pulls up a holographic map. Red markers dot the Lower District and Undercity like a disease. "We need that intelligence. Every faction safehouse you can identify, every tunnel entrance, every weapons cache. In return, Meridian guarantees your safety, your stack, and a permanent Highcity residency."

She leans in. "And if you're thinking about switching sides again — don't. We have your biometrics on file. Every drone in the city has your face loaded as a priority friendly. One flag change and they switch you to priority target. You would not make it two blocks."

text.text: You read the room with practiced eyes. Rask is scared. The tactical displays show more red than blue. Meridian is losing ground in the Lower District and the Undercity, holding steady in Midcity, dominant only in Highcity. They need your intelligence because their own network is compromised. That's leverage. Dangerous leverage, but leverage.

choices.text: Provide faction intelligence — commit fully to Meridian
Feed them false information — sabotage from inside
"I need to see the battlefield myself before I give you anything."
Attack Commander Rask and fight your way out

act4_009.title: Jade's Truth
text: Jade's cigarette pauses halfway to her lips. For the first time since you have known her, the mask slips. Not much. A millimeter of vulnerability showing through the cracks in a professional's armor.
"My daughter had a stack," she says quietly. "Meridian's basic package. I saved for six years to get her one. She was nine when they installed it. I thought I was protecting her."
She takes a long drag. Lets it out slow.

"LATENCY took her three years ago. I didn't know what it was then. One day my daughter was my daughter. The next day she was — still my daughter, but different. Subtle things. Preferences changed. Opinions shifted. She started talking about Meridian like they were saints. Started saying I should get registered. I thought it was just growing up."

"Then I found the data. Found out what LATENCY really was. Found out they'd rewritten her. My girl's personality, her real one, was overwritten by corporate loyalty programming and there was no backup of who she used to be."
She stubs the cigarette out on the rusted bench. "She's up in Highcity now. Works for Meridian PR. Doesn't remember me. Doesn't want to."
"So when you ask what's in it for me — I want that company to burn. I want Voss to watch it burn. And then I want him to burn too. That is what is in it for me."
She stands. "Now take the chip and go save what's left of this city. I've got a war to profiteer from."

choices.text: Take the chip. Head to the Undercity to disarm Ashfall charges.
Take the chip. Head to Midcity and fight.
Take the chip. Storm Highcity.

act4_010.title: Ember's Warning
text: "Close it?" Ember laughs, and the sound reverberates off the cavern walls in ways that defy the geometry of the space. "You cannot close a door from one side when something on the other side is holding it open."
She walks around the rift, trailing one hand through the air near its edge. Where her fingers pass, the violet light intensifies.

"The stack network was built on void-resonance technology. Meridian's founders discovered this cavern sixty years ago. They found the markings, deciphered enough to understand the principle — consciousness can be encoded as patterns in void energy. That is what a cortical stack really is. A tiny piece of the void, caged in metal, holding a human mind in a space that was never meant for human minds."

"Millions of stacks. Millions of tiny void anchors. All of them pulling at the fabric between here and there. And now, with the war, with the stacks crashing and burning and overloading — the fabric is tearing."
She turns to face you. The glow in her eyes is brighter now.

"If the war continues, if enough stacks are destroyed or activated or overwhelmed at once, this rift will expand. It will consume the Underground first. Then the Undercity. Then everything above it. Not destruction — absorption. Everything that passes through becomes part of the void. Conscious, aware, and trapped in a dimension with no physical form. Forever."
"You have hours. Maybe less. Stop the war, or the war becomes irrelevant because there won't be a city left to fight over."

text.text: "The kill code," Ember says, staring at you. "When you crashed the stack network, you released every consciousness stored in those stacks simultaneously. Millions of minds, dumped into the void resonance field at once. That is what tore this rift open. Your liberation of the city is what might destroy it." The accusation in her voice cuts deeper than any blade.

choices.text: Go stop the war in the Undercity — save who you can
Head to Midcity — the heaviest fighting needs to stop
Storm Highcity — Voss can order a ceasefire
Try to close the rift yourself

act4_011.title: Undercity — The Drowning Tunnels
text: The south tunnels are filling. Water pours through a ruptured main, ankle-deep and rising fast, brown with rust and sewage. Emergency lighting casts everything in sickly yellow. The sound is deafening — the roar of water, the screams of civilians, the distant percussion of explosions above transmitting through the rock like a giant's heartbeat.

Three hundred people are packed into the south gallery. Families. Children. Old people who can barely stand. They pressed into the Undercity because the streets above were a kill zone, and now the tunnels are becoming a tomb. The water is at your knees.

A man with a shattered leg is propped against the wall, holding his daughter above the water line with both arms. A woman is trying to build a barricade from debris to slow the flooding, stacking crates and broken furniture against the current. It is not working. The water does not care about barricades.

Behind you, the tunnel forks. Left leads deeper — older infrastructure, pre-war construction, possibly a drainage route. Right leads to a maintenance shaft that surfaces in the Midcity commercial district, two blocks from Meridian's forward base.
The water reaches your waist. You have minutes, not hours.

text.text: Your hands read the stone like braille. The left tunnel — you can feel the draft. There is airflow, which means there is open space beyond the flooding. The old drainage system runs below this level. If you can find the access point and open the valves, the water has somewhere to go. But the access points were sealed decades ago. Breaking through will take strength and time you may not have.

The ruptured main is visible where the ceiling has collapsed — a pipe thick as your torso, split along a weld seam. The flow is massive but the break is localized. If you could force something into the gap, a beam, a sheet of metal, anything strong enough, you might reduce the flow enough to buy time.

choices.text: Go left — find the old drainage system and redirect the water
Go right — evacuate everyone through the maintenance shaft
Try to block the ruptured main with debris
The water is too high. Save yourself.

act4_012.title: Undercity — The Old Drainage
text: The left tunnel drops steeply. Water cascades down the slope in a brown waterfall, and you fight your way against it, bracing against the walls, boots sliding on slick stone. The tunnel narrows until your shoulders scrape both sides. Then it opens.

A junction. Pre-war infrastructure, just as you hoped. The walls are ancient concrete, cracked but holding. Massive valve wheels protrude from the walls at three points, each one controlling a drainage gate that feeds into the deep aquifer below the city. The valves are seized with decades of corrosion. Rust has welded them shut.

The water is chest-deep here and still rising. You can hear the civilians screaming behind you, the sound funneled down the tunnel. A child is crying. The echoes make it sound like dozens of children crying.

You grab the nearest valve wheel. It does not move. Your arms shake with the effort. Rust flakes off under your grip but the mechanism beneath is frozen solid. You need more force, or more hands, or more time, and you have none of these things.
Then you hear footsteps behind you. Rust — the Iron Vanguard salvage specialist, a massive figure in welded-together armor — wades through the tunnel with a crowbar the length of your arm.
"Zero sent me," she says. "Figured you'd need muscle." She jams the crowbar into the valve wheel and pulls. Metal screams against metal. "On three. One. Two."

text.text: Your combined strength is barely enough. The valve screams, shrieks, and then turns — one inch, two, three. Water begins to drain through the gate beneath your feet with a sound like a giant swallowing. The level drops. Slowly at first, then faster.

choices.text: Keep turning the valves — drain all three gates
One gate is enough — go back and evacuate the civilians now

act4_013.title: Undercity — Evacuation Run
text: The maintenance shaft is a vertical climb — a metal ladder bolted to a concrete tube that rises forty meters to a hatch in the Midcity commercial district. For an able-bodied adult, it is a hard climb. For the people you are trying to save, many of them injured, elderly, carrying children, it is nearly impossible.

You organize them into groups. Strongest climbers first to secure the top. Then families with children, passed up hand to hand along a human chain on the ladder. Then the wounded, hauled up on improvised rope slings made from clothing and wire. The old man with the shattered leg screams when they lift him but he does not let go.

You stay at the bottom. The water is at your chest and rising. Each group that goes up takes time you are running out of. You count heads. Two hundred and sixty. Two hundred and seventy. Two hundred and eighty.

The last group is climbing when Meridian sec-teams breach the south tunnel. Six armored figures with helmet-mounted lights cutting through the flood water, pulse rifles raised. They were hunting for faction fighters in the tunnels and they found three hundred civilians and you.
"ON YOUR KNEES! HANDS VISIBLE! EVERYONE DOWN!" The lead trooper's voice is amplified to painful volume. His weapon light pins you against the ladder.
Twenty people are still below you on the ladder. If you fight, the crossfire will kill them. If you surrender, Meridian takes everyone.

text.text: The Ashfall detonation codes burn in your pocket like a physical weight. If Meridian finds them on you, Voss loses his leverage and you lose the only insurance the Undercity has against total destruction.

choices.text: Surrender to protect the remaining civilians
Fight — six against one in a flooded tunnel
Bluff: "Protocol Ashfall is compromised. Stand down or Voss loses everything."

act4_014.title: Undercity — The Tide Turns
text: The water recedes. Not fast — there is too much of it for that — but fast enough. It drops from chest to waist, waist to knee, knee to ankle. The drainage gates are swallowing the flood and the civilians in the south gallery are no longer drowning. They are standing in mud and wreckage, shaking, crying, alive.

Rust leans against the tunnel wall, breathing hard, the crowbar still in her massive hands. Her welded armor is streaked with rust-brown water. She looks at you and nods once. That is all the celebration the Iron Vanguard deals in.

Zero arrives from the north tunnel with a fresh squad of volunteers and a crate of supplies scavenged from a destroyed Meridian supply convoy. She takes one look at the gallery — three hundred people, cold, scared, but breathing — and her composure finally breaks. Tears cut channels through the grime on her face. She wipes them away with the back of her hand and starts triaging the wounded.

"You saved them," she says to you between patients. "But the Undercity isn't safe. Meridian has sec-teams sweeping every tunnel. And there's something else — Kira's people intercepted a transmission. Something called Protocol Ashfall. She says it's bad. She says it's end-of-the-world bad."
The gallery is a temporary victory. The war is still raging above. And the clock on Ashfall is ticking.

text.text: You already know about Ashfall. The thermobaric charges in the support columns. The chip Jade gave you contains the detonation codes and locations. You could disarm them — if you can reach them before Voss decides to trigger the protocol.

choices.text: Stay in the Undercity and disarm the Ashfall charges
Head to Midcity — join the main battle
Push upward to Highcity — end this at the source
Rally the Undercity survivors into a fighting force

act4_015.title: Undercity — Fight in the Flood
text: You move before they finish shouting. The water is your ally — it slows them as much as it slows you, and their heavy armor becomes an anchor. You grab the nearest piece of floating debris, a length of metal pipe, and drive it into the lead trooper's faceplate. The impact cracks the visor and he staggers back, his rifle discharging into the ceiling. Concrete rains down.

The second trooper fires. The pulse round burns past your ear so close you feel the heat, and hits the wall behind you in a shower of sparks. You are already moving, using the chaos, the water, the poor visibility. You close the distance to the second trooper and drag him under the surface. His armor fills with water and he thrashes, panicking.

The remaining four open fire. In the confined tunnel, the sound is unbearable. Pulse rounds hit water and turn it to steam. The civilians on the ladder are screaming. A round hits the wall inches from the climbing old man.

You fight with everything you have. Pipe, fists, the flooded tunnel itself. Two more go down. The fifth trooper's rifle jams and you break his arm through his armor. The sixth — the sixth puts a round through your left shoulder.
The pain is white. Total. Your arm goes dead. You fall against the wall, and the trooper levels his rifle at your face.
Then a crowbar comes out of the darkness behind him and removes his helmet and most of what was under it. Rust stands in the flood water, breathing hard, splattered with things that are not rust.
"You're welcome," she says.

text.text: Rust hauls you upright with one hand. The wound in your shoulder is bleeding freely but the pulse round cauterized part of it on the way through. You will live, if infection does not set in, if you find medical supplies, if the next fight does not finish what this one started. A lot of ifs.

choices.text: Finish the evacuation despite your wound
You're bleeding out. Sit down. Let someone else take over.

act4_016.title: Midcity — The Burning Boulevard
text: Midcity is hell. The commercial district that once hummed with consumer traffic is now a warzone measured in burning storefronts and overturned vehicles. The boulevard is a no-man's-land — Meridian security forces hold the east side from behind fortified positions in the corporate offices, while a combined force of Ash Collective fighters and Iron Vanguard heavies hold the west side from the ruins of a collapsed parking structure.

Between them, the street is a killing field. Drone wreckage litters the road. Bodies in various uniforms and no uniforms at all are scattered where they fell. A public holographic display, somehow still functioning, cycles through Meridian advertisements over the carnage: MERIDIAN STACK SERVICES — LIVE FOREVER.

The air is thick with smoke and the chemical smell of pulse weapon discharge. Every few seconds, a burst of fire erupts from one side or the other, answered immediately. The rhythm of it is almost mechanical — shoot, duck, reload, shoot again. A war of attrition that both sides are losing.

Kira finds you in the rubble of a side street. The Ash Collective lieutenant has a bandage wrapped around her head, blood seeping through at the temple, and she is carrying a Meridian pulse rifle she clearly took from someone who no longer needed it.

"About time. We've been pinned here for six hours. Meridian has heavy drones on the rooftops, automated gun platforms. We can't push east and they can't push west. Meanwhile the city is burning around both of us."

She pulls up a cracked datapad with a rough tactical map. "Three options. We push through the boulevard head-on — suicide, but it would break their line. We flank through the sewer access two blocks north — slower, might work. Or we pull back and let them have Midcity."

text.text: You recognize two of the Meridian officers behind the eastern barricade from the Forward Command briefing. If they recognize you, they will know you defected. Your cover, such as it was, lasts only as long as you stay on the west side of this boulevard.

The automated gun platforms on the rooftops are networked — you can see the telltale signal patterns in your HUD. Meridian's field encryption is good but you have cracked better. If you can access a terminal, you might be able to turn their own guns against them. Or at least shut them down.

choices.text: "We push the boulevard. Head-on. Give me a weapon."
"Flank through the sewers. Slow and smart."
"I can hack their gun platforms. Get me to a terminal."
"Pull back. Midcity isn't worth dying for."

act4_017.title: Midcity — The Sewer Flank
text: The sewer access is two blocks north, hidden behind a dumpster in an alley that stinks of chemical waste. Kira sends eight fighters with you — Ash Collective scouts who move through confined spaces like they were born underground. Which, given that most of them grew up in the Undercity, they practically were.

The sewers beneath Midcity are cleaner than the Undercity tunnels but no less oppressive. Prefabricated concrete sections, well-lit by emergency strips, with regular maintenance access points marked in corporate yellow. You move in single file, weapons up, stepping over puddles that reflect the emergency lights like pools of orange fire.

The plan is to surface behind Meridian's eastern line, in the maintenance basement of the corporate office they are using as a stronghold. Hit them from behind while Kira leads a diversionary push on the boulevard. Simple. Clean. The kind of plan that works perfectly until reality gets involved.

Reality gets involved at the third junction. A Meridian sec-team is already in the sewers. Eight of them, same number as your squad, moving in the opposite direction with the same idea you had — flanking through the sewers to hit the west side from behind.
You meet face-to-face at an intersection. For one frozen second, both groups stare at each other across ten meters of sewer tunnel. Then everyone starts shooting at once.

The confined space turns the firefight into slaughter on both sides. Pulse rounds ricochet off concrete walls. One of your scouts takes a round to the throat and drops without a sound. A Meridian trooper catches a shotgun blast that folds him in half.

text.text: Your reflexes save you. You drop flat half a second before a pulse round passes through the space your head occupied, then roll behind a support column. From cover, you pick your shots. Controlled. Precise. Two troopers go down.

The Meridian squad leader recognizes you. His eyes go wide behind his visor. 'That's the asset! The Core operative! They're a traitor!' The word echoes off the sewer walls and your Ash Collective squadmates look at you with sudden, dangerous suspicion.

choices.text: Push through the ambush — keep moving toward the objective
Fall back to the boulevard — the flank is compromised
Grab a fallen trooper's radio and call in false orders to Meridian

act4_018.title: Midcity — The Boulevard Charge
text: The charge across the boulevard is everything Kira warned it would be. Suicide with extra steps. But you are out of time and out of options and the fighters on both sides are out of patience.

You go first. Somebody has to. The first twenty meters are the worst — open ground, no cover, running directly at entrenched positions that have been fortified for six hours. Pulse rounds cut the air around you in bright lines. The asphalt under your feet cracks and bubbles where rounds hit. A drone above you adjusts its targeting and you dodge left as its cannon tears a line through the road surface where you just were.

Behind you, the assault force follows. Iron Vanguard heavies with makeshift shields made from vehicle doors, absorbing fire while Ash Collective fighters sprint between them. Three go down in the first ten seconds. Then four. Then you lose count because counting the dead while running is a good way to join them.

You reach the Meridian barricade. It is made of overturned security vehicles and sandbags, and the troopers behind it are firing as fast as their weapons cycle. You vault the barricade with a sound that comes out of your throat like an animal and land among them.

Close quarters. No room for rifles. Fists and elbows and the butt of a pulse pistol against helmet visors. A trooper grabs you from behind and you slam your head backward into his faceplate. Something cracks — his visor or your skull, you will determine later which.
When it ends, you are standing in a Meridian barricade that is now yours. Seven Meridian troopers are down. Eleven of your fighters are dead. Kira arrives with the rest and looks at the cost.
"We took the line," she says. Her voice is hollow. Eleven dead. Eleven names she will have to remember.

text.text: Your hands are bloody to the wrist. Not all of it is yours. During the melee you tore a trooper's helmet off with your bare hands, a feat that should have been impossible given the magnetic clamps. Adrenaline is a hell of a drug. Your body will charge interest on this later.

choices.text: Press the attack — push into Meridian's forward command
Fortify the position and tend the wounded
Leave Kira to hold the line — you need to reach Highcity

act4_019.title: Midcity — Turning Their Guns
text: The rooftop terminal is hidden behind a ventilation unit on the fourth floor of the corporate office. Getting here cost two more fighters and a knife wound across your ribs that you are actively trying not to think about. The Meridian automated gun platforms hum on their mounts above you, scanning for targets with mechanical patience.

The terminal is military-grade encrypted. Meridian does not use civilian security on their weapon systems. The encryption layers are nested — break one and the next is harder, break that and the third adapts to your methods. It is the kind of system designed to be uncrackable.

You crack it in four minutes. Not because you are better than the system — because you recognize the architecture. It is based on the same framework as the Meridian Core systems you infiltrated in Act 3. Same designers, same logic, same blind spots. They never patched the vulnerabilities you exploited because they never knew you exploited them.

The gun platforms accept your override codes. Four rooftop cannons, each one capable of shredding a vehicle in seconds, rotate on their mounts. Their targeting systems blink — red to green. Enemy to friendly. They are yours.
You point them at the Meridian barricade on the boulevard below.
"Kira," you say into your comm. "Get your people back from the line. Now."
"Why?"
"Because I'm about to delete their barricade."

The guns fire. The sound is like the sky falling. The Meridian fortification below disintegrates in a storm of hypervelocity rounds that turn sandbags to sand and vehicles to shrapnel. When the guns stop, there is nothing left of the eastern line but smoking craters and the moaning of the wounded.

text.text: While you are in their system, you pull everything you can. Troop deployments, supply routes, communication codes, and — there it is — a reference to Protocol Ashfall with a countdown timer. Eleven hours. Voss has already initiated the sequence. The thermobaric charges under the Undercity will detonate in eleven hours unless someone stops it.

choices.text: The boulevard is ours. Push to Highcity.
Download the Ashfall data and head to the Undercity to disarm the charges
Hold Midcity and try to rally all factions for a coordinated push

act4_020.title: Midcity — Kira's Decision
text: With the boulevard secured, the Midcity commercial district belongs to the resistance — for now. Kira sets up a command post in the ruins of a coffee shop, its windows blown out and its counter converted to a map table. Fighters from three different factions — Ash Collective, Iron Vanguard, a handful of Ghost Saints who materialized from nowhere — crowd into the space.

Kira looks at the tactical map and her jaw tightens. "We hold Midcity but we don't control it. Meridian still has the eastern commercial blocks, the transit hub, and they have drones. A lot of drones. We can't advance without air cover and we don't have any."
She looks at you. The bandage on her head is soaked through with blood. She has not let anyone change it.

"I have enough fighters to do one thing. One. I can push east and try to link up with the Undercity evacuation routes — protect the civilians. Or I can push north toward Highcity and try to take the fight to Voss. I can't do both."
"If we go east, people live but Voss stays in power and this war goes on forever. If we go north, we might end it, but the civilians in the Undercity are on their own."
She puts both hands flat on the map. "You've been making the calls that got us this far. So make this one."

text.text: You already saved three hundred people in the south tunnels. But there are thousands more. The Undercity is a maze and Meridian is sweeping it systematically. Every hour without support is measured in bodies.

choices.text: "East. Protect the civilians. That's what we're fighting for."
"North. We end this. Take the fight to Voss."
"Split the force. You go east, I go north alone."
"Neither. We dig in and negotiate. Force a ceasefire."

act4_021.title: Highcity — The Ascent
text: The transit bridge to Highcity is a kilometer of exposed steel and reinforced glass suspended two hundred meters above the Midcity rooftops. In peacetime, it carries the wealthy from their towers to their offices in climate-controlled comfort. Today it is a gauntlet.

Meridian has fortified both ends. Guard posts with automated turrets, blast doors rated to withstand military ordnance, and a drone screen thick enough to darken the sky above the bridge. The Lower District can burn, Midcity can fall, but Highcity is inviolate.
You approach from the Midcity side. The blast door is sealed. The turrets are active. A holographic projection of Director Voss loops his recorded smile above the doorway.

There is another way. The maintenance gantry beneath the bridge runs the full kilometer in open air. No turrets, no blast doors — just a narrow walkway with waist-high railings and a two-hundred-meter drop to the burning streets below.

Mako’s voice crackles through your comm: "There’s a third option. Service elevator on the south pylon. Manual. But it opens directly into the Highcity security atrium. Into the teeth of their defense."

text.text: The maintenance gantry is a death trap for anyone without exceptional balance. The walkway is less than a meter wide in places, the railings rusted to structural failure, and the wind at this height staggers you. But you have done harder things.

Elven balance. Centuries of genetic memory encoded in your inner ear, inherited from ancestors who walked branches thinner than this gantry above drops deeper than this one. Your body was built for heights that make humans weep.

choices.text: Take the maintenance gantry — the long, exposed crossing
Take the service elevator — fast but into heavy resistance
Assault the blast door directly

act4_022.title: Highcity — Walk Above the Fire
text: The gantry sways. Not a lot — a centimeter, maybe two — but at this height those centimeters feel like the world tilting. You grip the railing and it shifts under your hand. You stop gripping the railing.

Below you, the city burns. Midcity is a patchwork of fire and shadow. From up here the fighting looks almost peaceful — tiny flashes of pulse weapons, the slow drift of drones, ant-sized people killing and dying on streets you walked this morning.
Halfway across, a drone finds you. A Meridian surveillance unit, hovering twenty meters off with its camera on your face. Three seconds of scanning. Then it transmits.

Thirty seconds later, two combat drones rise from below the bridge. Weapons pods already glowing. There is nowhere to hide. Nowhere to run. A meter-wide walkway, two armed drones closing from opposite directions.

text.text: Your targeting system locks the drones, calculating trajectories. You could drop one before it fires. But the second reaches you three seconds later, and there is no cover on this gantry.

Structural supports every fifty meters — vertical beams connecting the gantry to the bridge above. The nearest is close enough to reach. Behind it, the drones lose their firing angle. You are trying to survive for three seconds. Three seconds is enough for something very stupid.

choices.text: Shoot the surveillance drone before combat drones arrive
Drop off the gantry and hang from the underside
Run. Full sprint. Reach the Highcity end before they fire.
Stand your ground. You are not going to make it.

act4_023.title: Highcity — The Elevator Ambush
text: The service elevator is a cage of metal and silence. It rises with agonizing slowness, the manual crank mechanism loud enough to announce your arrival to anyone listening.

Anyone is listening. The doors open onto eight pulse rifles held by Vanguard Division — genetically optimized, neurally enhanced, running combat stacks for reflex speeds no baseline human matches. Matte black armor, single chrome stripe. They do not speak.
Behind them, the Highcity security atrium: marble, glass, crystal chandeliers, classical music, a running fountain. Pristine. Untouched. The contrast with the hell below is nauseating.

A woman in a tailored suit steps forward. "Welcome to Highcity. Director Voss offers a choice. Surrender and be detained comfortably. Or don’t, and learn why Vanguard Division has never lost a close-quarters engagement."

text.text: The woman’s smile sharpens. ‘The prodigal asset returns. Voss predicted you’d climb back. Your biometrics are still flagged friendly. Walk these halls freely. Just remember which side you chose.’
You read the formation. Eight rifles, staged frontal. The elevator is a choke point — they cannot bring all eight to bear. The woman stands too close to their line of fire. A vulnerability.

choices.text: Surrender. Live to fight another way.
Fight through Vanguard Division
"I want to talk to Voss. Face to face."
Grab the woman as a hostage

act4_024.title: Highcity — Tower Warfare
text: Fighting through Highcity is nothing like Midcity or the Undercity. Wide spotless corridors, marble walls, every surface reflecting your image. The elite guards communicate through combat stacks in silence, coordinating without words.

You ascend floor by floor. Residential floors: terrified civilians who have never witnessed violence off a screen. A woman throws a crystal vase at your head. A man offers his entire credit balance. A child stares through a cracked door with eyes that will never un-see what they see.
Corporate floors are worse. Executives barricaded with private security. One office is booby-trapped — a directional charge kills two fighters who breach the door.
By the executive level, you have lost count of the dead. Theirs. Yours. The distinction blurs.
Voss’s office is at the top. The final door is unlocked.

text.text: Your datapad vibrates. Ember: ‘THE RIFT GROWS.’ An image — the void rift, now twice the size. Purple light bleeds through rock, visible from the Undercity tunnels. Time is running out.

choices.text: Walk through the unlocked door and face Voss
It is a trap. Find another way in.

act4_025.title: Highcity — Director Voss
text: The office is the size of a house. Floor-to-ceiling windows on three sides show a city dying. Fires in every district. Smoke on the horizon. Weapons fire reflected off the cloud layer like heat lightning.

Director Harlan Voss stands at the window, tumbler in hand. Smaller than he appears on broadcasts. Older. His smile is engineered warmth, but his eyes are empty — the way eyes get when the person behind them has accepted something terrible.

"You made it. I was not certain you would. The odds were against you at every stage." He sips his drink. "But here you are. The hero of the Lower District, covered in blood and righteous fury, standing in my office."

He sets the glass on an obsidian desk. "So. What now? Kill me and the corporation fragments — a dozen factions with stack tech and no infrastructure to maintain it. Millions of stacks degrade, millions of minds lost. Or don’t kill me, and I trigger Ashfall and bury the Undercity."
"There are no good choices left. The good choices died in peacetime and nobody noticed."

text.text: "You destroyed the stack network," Voss says, and real emotion crosses his face. ‘My wife’s backup was in that network. My daughter’s. Thirty years of work, and you killed them with a keystroke.’ He looks at his hands. ‘Perhaps we are both monsters.’

You hold up Jade’s data chip. ‘I have the Ashfall codes. Every charge location. Trigger the protocol and I broadcast them — every faction races to disarm.’ Something flickers behind those empty eyes. Surprise. Respect. Fear.

choices.text: Kill Voss. End the head of the snake.
"Call off Ashfall. Order a ceasefire. I’ll let you live."
"The void rift. We have a bigger problem than this war."
Take his stack technology. Become the new Meridian.

act4_026.title: Underground — The Awakening
text: The Underground is changing. Tunnels pulse with violet light in a heartbeat rhythm. Temperature has dropped to where breath fogs. Ice crystals form on metal in patterns like alien writing.
The void rift has expanded — floor to ceiling, wall to wall. Through it, things move. Shapes made of absence. Shadows without a source, flowing like spilled ink across the cavern floor.

Ember stands at the edge, arms raised, rigid. Violet glow has consumed her eyes. She chants in a language that predates human speech. Visible ripples in the air. She is trying to hold the rift. She is losing.
"It feeds on conflict," she gasps. "Every death up there — the rift drinks it. If the membrane breaks, this district goes first. Then the city. Then everything within a hundred kilometers."
A shadow-shape detaches from the rift and flows to you. No face, but presence. It stops at your feet and waits.

text.text: The shadow recognizes you, circles your feet like a returning cat. You understand the shapes. They are refugees, fleeing something worse on the other side. They are terrified.

Your shadow-blood answers. For a moment you see both sides — the cavern, and the vast nothing beyond. The nothing is full. Minds without bodies, consciousnesses without form, trapped in eternal awareness without sensation. Worse than death.

choices.text: Help Ember hold the rift
Enter the void rift
Stop the war above — the only way to close it
Use the rift as a weapon against Meridian

act4_027.title: Underground — Holding the Line Between Worlds
text: You stand beside Ember and reach for the rift. Not physically. Something deeper resonates with the void energy — maybe the stack technology, maybe something older.
The effort pulls at your sense of self. Memories loosen. Your name becomes uncertain. Your past becomes a story about someone else.
Ember screams in effort. The rift shudders. Contracts by a centimeter.
Then a detonation shakes the cavern. The war above. The burst of death feeds the rift like oxygen feeds flame. It expands by a meter. Void-shapes flood through.
Ember collapses. Blood from her nose, ears. Eyes dim to grey. "I can’t. Not alone. Not while they keep killing each other."
The void-shapes fill the cavern. Confused. Terrified. Terrified things with power they don’t understand are the most dangerous things in any world.

text.text: A terrible thought: the Ashfall charges. Thermobaric detonations would bury the rift under millions of tons. Would not close it — but contain it. Along with everyone down here.

choices.text: Go topside and end the war
Open the rift wider
Sacrifice yourself to seal the rift
Trigger Ashfall charges to bury it

act4_028.title: Underground — The Price of Closure
text: You step into the rift. Not through it. Into it. Through implies a destination. Into implies absorption.

The void takes your body first — dissolving into patterns of energy you feel but cannot move. Then your senses. Sight, replaced by spacial awareness without light. Sound, replaced by vibrations in a medium beyond air. Touch. Taste. Smell. Gone.
Your consciousness remains. The cruelty of the void — absolute fidelity of awareness. You are still you. Always will be. But no longer anywhere, any-when, any-thing.
The rift closes behind you. Your sacrifice worked. The tear seals with a finality that echoes across dimensions. Void-shapes on the other side weaken, fade.
Ember screams your name. A vibration in the nothing. She will tell the others.
You are not dead. Not alive. Something else. Aware. Eternal. Alone.
At least the city is safe.

act4_029.title: Underground — Unleashing the Void
text: You do not close the rift. You open it.
You reach into the tear and pull. The rift screams and tears wider. Violet light floods the cavern, the tunnels, bleeds upward through rock.
Void-shapes pour through by the thousands. They flood every tunnel, climb through cracks in the earth, through infrastructure, through foundations, reaching for the surface.
In the streets, void erupts. Shadows boil from the ground. Not destroying — absorbing. Buildings phase out of existence. People touched by the wave stop, frozen, consciousnesses pulled into the void.
A district consumed in minutes. The war stops — not by choice, but because nothing remains to fight over. Where the Undercity stood: a crater of non-space. Violet at the edges. Nothing at the center.
Ember is gone. Civilians, fighters, refugees, children. All conscious. All trapped.
You stand at the edge and understand what you have done.

text.text: The void-shapes recognize you as liberator. They gather — a court of shadows bowing to a chosen king. Their hunger: more rifts. The whole world.

act4_030.title: Midcity — Breakout from Meridian Command
text: You hit Commander Rask before she finishes her sentence. Fist to chrome jaw — military-grade titanium, barely moved. But the surprise buys one second.
Rask recovers fast. Crushing grip on your wrist, combat twist for your shoulder. You roll with it, convert her force to momentum, elbow to the back of her neck. She staggers. You run.

Alarms. Shouted orders. Pulse rifles charging. You sprint through the converted complex, dodging sandbag positions. A round hits the floor — ceramic shrapnel catches your cheek, ear to jaw. You do not slow down.
Through a broken window, into the street. War zone chaos is your advantage now — too much confusion to track one runner.
Rask’s voice on the loudspeakers: "All units, the Core asset has gone hostile. Priority target. Lethal force authorized."
Every drone in the city just added your face to their kill list.

choices.text: Find Kira and the resistance in Midcity
Go to ground. Disappear.
Head for the Undercity

act4_031.title: Ending #31 — The Warlord of the Undercity
text: You do not leave the Undercity. You claim it.

In the weeks that follow, as the surface factions exhaust themselves in pointless combat, you consolidate power below. The tunnel networks become your domain. The refugees become your people. The weapons caches become your arsenal. You build what no one thought possible — a functioning society beneath the ruins.

Mako arms your militia. Zero runs the hospitals. Rust leads the patrols. The Undercity, always despised, always forgotten, becomes the strongest territory in the metropolitan zone. Not because of military might — because you control the water mains, the power conduits, the transit tunnels. Everything the surface needs runs through your ground.
They come to negotiate eventually. Meridian. The factions. What remains of the municipal government. They come because they have no choice.

You sit in a chamber carved from living rock, lit by bioluminescent moss cultivated by the dwarf engineers, and you receive them like a sovereign receiving petitioners. You are not a hero. Heroes die in wars. You are something more practical. More permanent.
You are the Warlord of the Undercity. And the city above will learn to knock before entering.
The throne was never offered. You built it from the rubble with your own hands.

act4_032.title: Ending #32 — The Alliance
text: It takes three days of negotiation, two assassination attempts, and one bar fight that nearly becomes a massacre. But you do it. You bring the Iron Vanguard and the Ash Collective to the same table, forge an alliance sealed in blood and pragmatism, and point them at the common enemy.

The combined assault on Meridian’s Highcity positions is devastating. Iron Vanguard heavies breach the transit bridge while Ash Collective infiltrators disable the drone network from inside. Ghost Saints — unbidden, unasked — appear in the Meridian executive suites like ghosts made of knives and finish what the assault started.
Voss flees. His board of directors scatters. Meridian Stack Services, the corporation that sold immortality and slavery in the same package, collapses in a single day.

The alliance holds for exactly long enough. Then the factions turn on each other, because that is what factions do. But the stack technology is free now. Open-source. Maintained by a collective of engineers and hackers who answer to no corporation.

You do not lead the new order. You brokered it, and that is enough. In the rebuilt Midcity, they name a street after you. You never walk down it. The street name is a monument to a person who no longer exists — the fighter. You are someone else now. Someone tired.
But the alliance held long enough. And sometimes long enough is all you get.

act4_033.title: Ending #33 — Protocol Ashfall
text: The charges detonate in sequence, north to south, a ripple of destruction that takes eleven seconds to cross the Undercity. You hear it before you feel it — a sound so low and so vast it is felt more than heard, a vibration that starts in your bones and does not stop.

The Undercity collapses. Support columns that have held millions of tons of city for a century shatter in cascading failure. The ceiling comes down not as one piece but as a wave, section after section folding inward, burying everything beneath in an avalanche of concrete, steel, and earth.

The tunnels. The refugees. The markets and the clinics and the makeshift schools. The children who played in the drainage channels. The old woman who sold noodles from a cart near junction seven. The man who sang opera in the south gallery because the acoustics were perfect. All of it. All of them.
Buried.

The surface trembles. Midcity cracks — buildings sag, streets split open, sinkholes swallow intersections. But it holds. Meridian’s engineers calculated the demolition precisely. The surface survives. Highcity stands untouched.

Director Voss watches from his tower as the dust settles. His city is saved. His company endures. The troublesome poor, the inconvenient refugees, the annoying resistance — buried under ten million tons of rubble.

You helped this happen. Whether you triggered the charges yourself, or fed Meridian the intelligence that made it possible, or simply failed to stop it in time — the result is the same. The Undercity is a grave. And you helped dig it.
Somewhere in the rubble, a child’s hand reaches upward through a gap in the debris. It does not move.

act4_034.title: Ending #34 — The Evacuation
text: You choose the civilians. Every time, you choose the civilians.

The evacuation takes eighteen hours. Eighteen hours of guiding thousands of people through flooded tunnels, past Meridian patrols, through collapsed sections that require climbing over rubble in the dark while children cry and the elderly struggle and the wounded bleed. Eighteen hours of holding flashlights and carrying people and lying to them that everything will be okay.

Zero works without stopping. She treats wounds by touch in total darkness, her hands steady, her voice calm, her heart breaking invisibly behind her professional composure. Rust carries three people at once through a flooded section, her massive frame the only thing keeping them above the waterline. Mako, who has no business being in these tunnels at his age, holds the rear guard with his ancient shotgun and does not let a single Meridian trooper through.

You lose people. Of course you lose people. A section of tunnel collapses and buries twelve. An elderly man sits down against a wall and refuses to move and dies there. A teenager gets separated in a junction and is never found. Each loss is a knife. Each loss makes the next step heavier.

But you reach Pier Nine. The old service exit opens onto the harbor, salt air and grey sky and rain. The first refugees step out into the open and some of them fall to their knees and some of them cry and some of them just stand there, breathing, because breathing in the open air is a miracle they did not expect to experience again.
Four thousand, three hundred and twelve people. You counted. You will always know the number.

Behind you, the Undercity groans as Ashfall detonates. The exit collapses seconds after the last person crawls through. If you had been one minute slower, if you had stopped to rest, if you had hesitated at any of the hundred decision points along the way — but you didn’t.
The city burns above. The war continues. But four thousand, three hundred and twelve people are alive who would not be without you. That is not victory. But it is not nothing.

act4_035.title: Ending #35 — Body Theft
text: Surrender was the wrong word. Acquisition was closer.

They do not detain you in a comfortable suite. They take you to a Meridian research lab in the sub-basement of Highcity Tower One, a facility that does not appear on any blueprint and is staffed by people whose names have been scrubbed from every database. The room is white. The chair is metal. The restraints are unnecessary because the paralytic they injected works faster than your reflexes.

A technician connects a cortical stack interface to the port at the base of your skull — the port you did not know you had, installed during one of a dozen medical procedures you underwent without full anesthesia records. Meridian has been preparing for this. Preparing for you.
"The consciousness transfer will take approximately four minutes," the technician says, reading from a tablet. "The host body has been prepared. Your current physical form will be recycled."
"Recycled," you repeat, because the word is obscene.
"Standard protocol. Director Voss has selected your replacement personally. A Meridian executive who lost their primary body in the riots. They need a new one. Yours has been rated suitable."

You fight the paralytic. Every fiber of your being fights it. But chemistry does not care about willpower, and the transfer begins. You feel yourself being copied — not moved, copied — your consciousness peeled from your brain like a label from a bottle and written onto a storage medium.
You wake up. New body. Shorter. Different hands. Different face in the mirror. Someone else’s scars, someone else’s muscle memory, someone else’s allergies. Your mind in a stranger’s flesh.
Somewhere in the city, your old body walks and talks and makes decisions. But it is not you making them. It is a Meridian executive wearing your face like a suit.
You are alive. You are someone else. And the person who took your body is living your life better than you did.

act4_036.title: Ending #36 — Consumed by the Void
text: The void takes the district. Not quickly — slowly, like a tide that does not recede. The edges of the rift expand outward in a circle, absorbing rock, metal, infrastructure, flesh. Everything it touches ceases to exist in physical space and begins to exist in void-space, conscious and screaming in a dimension where screaming is an abstract concept.
You triggered this. Whether by opening the rift wider or by failing to close it in time, the result is identical. A circle of nothing one kilometer across where the Underground used to be. Growing.

The war stops. Both sides stare at the crater and realize they have been fighting over a city that is being eaten by something older than humanity. The factions pull back. Meridian pulls back. Even Director Voss, watching from his tower, goes pale.

Ember is gone. Her last act was reaching for you, her violet eyes blazing, her mouth forming words you could not hear over the roar of reality tearing apart. She knew what was coming. She tried to warn everyone. Nobody listened.

The crater stabilizes. For now. But the edges glow violet at night, and sometimes, in the silence before dawn, you can hear voices coming from inside it. Not words. Not exactly. But something that wants to be heard. Something that remembers being human.
The city will never recover. The wound in reality is permanent. And you will spend the rest of your life knowing that you could have prevented it.
Some doors, once opened, cannot be closed.

act4_037.title: Ending #37 — The Impossible Ceasefire
text: They told you it was impossible. Five factions — Iron Vanguard, Ash Collective, Ghost Saints, the synth underground, and what remains of the municipal authority — have not agreed on anything since the city was founded. They have blood feuds older than you are. They have ideological differences that make compromise a four-letter word. They would rather die than sit in the same room.
You make them sit in the same room.

It takes charisma that borders on supernatural. You speak to the Iron Vanguard in the language of survival. To the Ash Collective in the language of justice. To the Ghost Saints in the language of profit. To the synths in the language of rights. To the municipal authority in the language of power. You tell each of them what they need to hear, and the miracle is that none of it is a lie.

The void rift is your leverage. You show them Ember’s data, the expanding tear between worlds, the timer counting down to catastrophe. You show them that their war is feeding it. That every bullet fired, every bomb detonated, every life taken widens the wound in reality.
"Stop fighting," you tell them, "or there won’t be a city left to fight over."

Director Voss, attending via hologram from his damaged tower, objects. Commander Rask, representing Meridian’s military arm, objects louder. But the other factions are listening. The void is real. The threat is real. And you are the only person in the room who has seen both sides of the rift and come back to talk about it.

The ceasefire holds for seventy-two hours. Long enough for Ember and a team of void-touched engineers to stabilize the rift. Long enough for the wounded to be treated. Long enough for the dead to be counted.

It does not hold forever. Nothing does. But you created something that has never existed in this city — a moment when everyone stopped killing each other and worked on the same problem. A moment of unity in a place built on division.

They will go back to fighting eventually. The factions, the corporations, the desperate and the powerful. But they will remember this. They will remember that it was possible. And maybe, next time, it will be easier.
Your CHA stat has been the most powerful weapon in this war. Words beat bullets. This time.

act4_038.title: Ending #38 — The Lone Wolf
text: You kill Director Harlan Voss alone, in his office, with his own letter opener.

It is not heroic. It is not dramatic. He reaches for something under his desk — a weapon, an alarm, the Ashfall trigger — and you move faster. The letter opener is obsidian, sharp as glass, heavy as judgment. It enters below his ribs and he makes a sound like a punctured tire and sits down hard in his executive chair.

He looks at you. The engineered smile is gone. The corporate mask is gone. What remains is an old man dying in a leather chair, bleeding onto an obsidian desk, watching his empire collapse in real-time on the holographic displays that surround him.
"The charges," he whispers. "Ashfall. Dead man’s switch. My vitals drop, it triggers automatically. Sixty minutes."

He might be lying. He is Meridian. Lying is in the corporate charter. But if he is telling the truth, you have one hour to find and disarm thermobaric charges buried across the Undercity before they detonate.
You leave his body in the chair and run. Sixty minutes. The clock starts now.

You reach the first charge with forty-seven minutes to spare. Jade’s data chip guides you to the second, the third, the fourth. You call in every contact you have — Mako, Kira, Zero, anyone with hands and courage. The disarmament becomes a city-wide sprint against physics.
The last charge is disarmed with ninety seconds on the timer. Ninety seconds between the Undercity surviving and the Undercity becoming a mass grave.

Voss is dead. Meridian is headless. The war sputters and dies without its architect. You walk out of the Highcity tower covered in blood that is not all yours and find a city that is broken, burning, traumatized — but alive.

They do not build statues for people who kill with letter openers. But the city breathes tonight because of what you did alone, in a room at the top of a tower, with a dead man’s switch counting down.

act4_039.title: Ending #39 — Protecting Pixel
text: You find Pixel in sector 9 drainage, exactly where the synth message said she would be. The synth child is huddled in a maintenance alcove, her synthetic skin streaked with grime, her optical sensors dimmed to conserve power. She is holding a dead cat — a real cat, biological, the kind that used to live in the Undercity before the war drove them all into hiding. The cat died of smoke inhalation. Pixel is holding it because she does not understand that holding it will not bring it back.

She looks up when she hears you. Her face — designed to approximate a seven-year-old human child, imperfect in the ways that make synths uncanny and heartbreaking — cycles through recognition, relief, and then fear.
"They’re coming," she says. "The loud ones. With the lights on their heads."

Meridian sec-teams. Sweeping the tunnels. The drainage sector is a dead end — one way in, one way out, and the way out leads directly into the sweep pattern. You can hear boots echoing. Flashlight beams cutting the darkness in the main tunnel.

You have a weapon. One pulse pistol, half-charged. Enough for maybe four shots. The sec-team will be six to eight troopers, armed, armored, and operating under standing orders to deactivate any unregistered synth on sight. Pixel is unregistered. Pixel is, in Meridian’s classification system, stolen property.
You could run. Leave the synth child and save yourself. Nobody would know. Nobody would blame you. She is a machine, technically. A sophisticated machine, but a machine.
You step in front of her and raise the pistol.

They come around the corner. Six of them. You fire twice and two go down. The third fires back and the round hits you in the chest. The impact is a nova of pain and you stagger but do not fall. You fire again. Another trooper drops.

The fourth round hits your leg. You go to one knee. Pixel screams behind you — a child’s scream, indistinguishable from human, because the engineers who built her understood that a child’s scream is a child’s scream regardless of the substrate.

You fire your last shot. Miss. The remaining troopers advance. You throw the empty pistol at the nearest one and grab a length of pipe from the drainage debris. You swing. Connect. Go down under return fire.
The last thing you hear is Pixel crying your name. The last thing you feel is small synthetic hands on your face, trying to hold you together.
A synth child lives. A human dies protecting her. In a city that prices consciousness by the credit and sells immortality by the installment, that equation makes no sense.
It did not need to make sense. It needed to be right.

act4_040.title: Ending #40 — The Ghost
text: You disappear.

Not dramatically. Not heroically. You simply stop being where people expect you to be. You drop your datapad in a storm drain. You change your clothes. You cut your hair with a knife in a public bathroom mirror and do not recognize the person staring back at you, and that is exactly the point.

The war continues without you. It was always going to continue without you. You were never the protagonist of this story — you were a person caught in the gears of a machine too large for any individual to stop. The factions fight. Meridian retaliates. The void rift grows or stabilizes or consumes, depending on variables you no longer influence.

You walk to the harbor. The docks are abandoned — the shipping companies evacuated when the fighting started. A cargo shuttle sits on its pad, fueled and forgotten, its navigation system still logged into a route that leads to the outer settlements. Places with no stacks, no corporations, no factions. Places where people still live small lives in small communities and die when their bodies decide it’s time.

You board the shuttle. You do not look back at the city. The skyline is smoke and fire and the distant glow of the void rift, and looking at it would require you to feel things you have decided not to feel anymore.

In the outer settlements, you become someone else. A farmer. A mechanic. A person who sleeps through the night and eats meals at regular intervals and does not wake up reaching for a weapon that is no longer there.
Years pass. The city rebuilds, or does not rebuild. You do not check. You planted a garden. You fixed a neighbor’s roof. You learned the names of the local birds.

Sometimes, in the moments before sleep, you hear the sounds of the tunnels. The water. The screams. Mako’s shotgun. Kira’s voice on the comm. Zero’s tears. You hear them and you let them pass through you like radio signals from a station you no longer tune into.
You are a ghost. You are free. Whether those are the same thing is a question you have decided not to answer.

act4_041.title: Ending #26 — Death on the Bridge
text: The blast door does not yield. The turrets do not miss.

You make it fifteen meters across the bridge before the automated targeting systems acquire you. Three turrets fire simultaneously — overlapping fields of fire designed to eliminate exactly the kind of desperate charge you are attempting. The first round hits you in the torso. The second hits your leg. The third is redundant.

You fall on the bridge, face-down on glass that offers a vertigo-inducing view of the burning city two hundred meters below. Your blood pools on the transparent surface, spreading in a dark circle that refracts the firelight from below into a corona of red and orange.
The turrets stop firing. You are no longer classified as a threat. The targeting systems return to standby mode, patient and indifferent.
The last thing you see is the city through the glass beneath you. Burning. Fighting. Dying. Exactly as it was before you tried to change it.
The bridge cleans itself. The blood is gone by morning. Nobody knows you died here.

act4_042.title: Ending #27 — Drowned in the Tunnels
text: The water wins.

You fight it. God, do you fight it. Swimming against a current that has the weight of a ruptured water main behind it, holding your breath while brown water fills the tunnel ceiling to ceiling, reaching for a surface that is no longer there.

Your lungs burn. Your arms slow. The cold seeps into your muscles and steals their function one fiber at a time. The water is not malicious — water does not hate. It simply occupies space and you are in the way.

The last air pocket collapses. The water closes over your head and does not recede. You breathe in because your body demands it, override impossible, and the water enters you and takes everything with clinical efficiency.

They find your body three days later when the tunnels are pumped dry. Face down in the mud, one hand reaching toward the junction where the drainage valve waited to be turned. Six meters short. You were six meters from saving everyone.
Zero cries when they bring your body up. She does not stop for a long time.

act4_043.title: Ending #28 — Killed in the Boulevard Charge
text: You make it halfway across the boulevard before the drone finds your range.

The combat drone’s cannon is designed for vehicle suppression. Using it on a person is like using a sledgehammer on an eggshell. The round does not kill you so much as redistribute you across twelve square meters of asphalt.

Kira sees it happen. She screams something — your name, a curse, a battle cry — and charges the Meridian line with a ferocity that terrifies both sides. She takes the barricade alone, fighting with the kind of abandon that comes from watching someone die and deciding that death has lost its leverage.
She wins the boulevard. She names the victory after you. It does not feel like a victory to anyone who was there.

Your body is recovered from the no-man’s land after the fighting stops. What remains of it is buried in Midcity, in a park that used to have trees, under a marker that reads only a name and a date. No title. No epitaph. The people who knew you decided that you would have preferred silence to sentiment.
They were right.

act4_044.title: Ending #29 — Lost to the Underground
text: The Underground takes you the way the ocean takes swimmers who go too far from shore — not all at once, but in increments so small you do not realize you are lost until the shore is gone.

You go deeper to investigate the void rift. Then deeper to find Ember. Then deeper because the tunnels shift and the way back is no longer where it was. The Underground is old. Older than the city, older than the people who built the city, older than the species that evolved into the people. And it does not want you to leave.
