The void energy saturates the rock. It gets into your thoughts. Your compass fails, then your datapad, then your sense of direction. You walk in circles that feel like straight lines. You find junctions you are certain you have never seen before, and yet your bootprints are already in the dust.

Days pass. Maybe. Time moves differently near the void. Your supplies run out. Your light dies. You walk in darkness so complete that your eyes manufacture hallucinations to fill the emptiness — colors, shapes, faces of people you knew, all of them dissolving when you reach for them.

You sit down because your legs decide for you that walking is finished. The rock is cold. The darkness is total. Somewhere in the distance, or perhaps very close, the void rift pulses in that heartbeat rhythm.
You close your eyes. There is no difference.
The Underground keeps you. Not with malice. Not with intent. It simply does not let go of things that wander into its depths. You are not the first. The bones say you will not be the last.

act4_045.title: Ending #30 — Executed by Meridian
text: Meridian does not hold trials. Trials imply a justice system, and justice is a service Meridian discontinued from its portfolio three fiscal quarters ago due to insufficient returns on investment.

They put you against a wall in a Highcity courtyard that was designed for corporate garden parties. The wall is white marble. The firing squad is Vanguard Division, six troopers in matte black, pulse rifles charged and leveled with mechanical precision. A Meridian executive reads a statement that uses words like ‘insurrection’ and ‘terrorism’ and ‘threat to shareholder value’ without apparent irony.
Director Voss watches from a balcony above. He does not smile. He does not gloat. He watches with the same flat attention he gives quarterly earnings reports. You are a line item being resolved.

They ask if you have final words. You consider several options. A defiant speech. A curse. A joke. In the end, you say nothing. Everything that needed saying was said in the tunnels and the streets and the burning buildings. Words are finished.
The squad fires. The marble wall behind you cracks. You fall.

In the Lower District, a screen flickers and your image appears briefly before Meridian’s censors kill the feed. But people saw it. People will remember. A face on a wall, a body falling, a corporation showing the city exactly what it does to those who resist.
Martyrs are more dangerous than soldiers. Meridian has just made the worst investment of its existence.

act4_046.title: Mako Falls
text: You hear about Mako on the comm. Kira’s voice, stripped of everything except the information: "Mako’s shop is gone. Meridian hit it with a guided munition. Direct strike. The building is rubble."
A pause. Static. Then: "He was inside. He didn’t evacuate. He was handing out weapons to anyone who came to the door. He didn’t stop when the drones came. He didn’t stop when they told him to stop."
Another pause. Longer. "They found his metal arm in the debris. Still gripping the shotgun."

The city loses people every day. The war eats them like fuel. But some losses tear a hole that the war cannot fill. Mako was a fixed point — everyone knew where his shop was, everyone knew he would be there, everyone knew that no matter how bad things got, the old man with the metal arm would have a weapon and a plan.
The fixed point is gone. The weapon is buried. The plan died with the man who made it.

You stand in the ruins of the street where the shop used to be and you feel something fundamental shift inside you. Not grief — grief comes later, in quiet moments, in the gaps between violence. What you feel now is colder. Harder. It is the understanding that this war will take everything from you, one piece at a time, until there is nothing left to take.

choices.text: Channel the rage. Fight harder.
This has to end. Go to Highcity and finish it.
Walk away. You cannot take any more.

act4_047.title: Kira’s Last Stand
text: Kira dies holding the boulevard.

You are not there when it happens. You learn about it afterward, from survivors who speak in the flat monotone of people describing events too large to process emotionally. Meridian counterattacked with armored transports and Vanguard Division shock troops. The west line buckled. Fighters fell back. Kira did not.

She held the barricade with six Ash Collective fighters who refused to leave her. They held it for forty-seven minutes against odds that would have made a military tactician weep. They held it until the ammunition ran out, and then they held it with debris and fists and the furious, burning certainty that the ground they stood on meant something.

When the transports rolled over the barricade, Kira detonated a charge she had been carrying since the battle started. The blast destroyed one transport, disabled another, and killed every Meridian trooper within fifteen meters.
It also killed Kira. And her six. And any hope of holding Midcity without reinforcements.
The bandage on her head was still there when they found her. She never did let anyone change it.

choices.text: She died for Midcity. Take it back in her name.
Midcity is lost. Fall back to the Undercity.
End this war before anyone else dies.

act4_048.title: Zero Down
text: Zero is hit by a stray round while treating a wounded child. The irony is surgical. She spent the entire war saving people, and a bullet meant for someone else finds her instead. It enters below her left shoulder blade and exits through her chest, and she falls across the child she was bandaging.

The child screams. Volunteers rush to Zero’s side and apply the same field medicine she taught them, using techniques she drilled into their hands during the desperate early hours of the evacuation. They pack the wound. They apply pressure. They talk to her in the calm, steady voice she always used with her patients.

She lives. Barely. The round missed her heart by two centimeters — two centimeters of luck in a war that has been notably short on the stuff. She will not fight again. She will not run again. She will spend weeks in recovery and years dealing with the damage.
But she will live. And the child she fell on is unharmed. And the volunteers she trained are still working, still saving, still doing what she showed them how to do.
Some legacies are measured in buildings. Hers is measured in hands that know how to stop bleeding.

choices.text: Stay with Zero. Make sure she pulls through.
She’s stable. The war won’t wait. Move.

act4_049.title: Jade’s Betrayal
text: Jade sold you out.

The Ashfall data chip was real. The detonation codes, the charge locations — all accurate. But Jade also sold a copy to Meridian, along with your location, your known associates, and a detailed timeline of your movements. She sold it for enough credits to buy passage off-world for herself and her rewritten daughter, whom she intends to kidnap from the Meridian PR department.
You discover this when a Meridian strike team arrives at exactly the wrong moment, armed with exactly the right intelligence. Jade’s information is thorough. She always was thorough.

The strike team misses you by minutes — you moved early, a paranoid habit from your old life that pays its dividend now. But three resistance fighters at the rendezvous are not as lucky. They die in a crossfire they never saw coming, because they trusted your judgement, and your judgement trusted Jade.

You find Jade’s farewell message on your datapad, encoded in a cipher only you would recognize: ‘Don’t take it personally. I told you I wanted Meridian to burn. I didn’t say I planned to burn with it. Good luck.’
Three people dead because you trusted an information broker in a war zone. The lesson costs exactly three lives.

choices.text: The data is still valid. Use it. Disarm the charges.
Everything is compromised. New plan. Storm Highcity.
Trust no one. Go alone.

act4_050.title: Ember’s Sacrifice
text: Ember steps into the rift before you can stop her.

She turns at the threshold — the edge between matter and void, between existence and whatever lies beyond it. The violet glow in her eyes is blinding now, brighter than the rift itself, as if her body has become a conduit for energies it was never designed to channel.
"I’ve spent my life studying the void," she says, and her voice carries harmonics from the other side. "Now I get to see it from inside. There are worse ways to go."
She looks at you. For one moment, the glow dims and she is just a woman — scared, determined, making a choice she cannot take back. "Close it behind me. Use the frequency. You’ll know which one."

She steps through. The rift shudders. Contracts. The void-shapes that poured through begin to weaken, their anchor dissolving. And in your mind, a frequency becomes clear — a tone, a vibration, a pattern that you can almost speak.
You speak it. The rift snaps shut like a wound healing in fast-forward. The violet light dies. The cavern goes dark.
Silence. The heartbeat rhythm is gone. The void is sealed. Ember is on the other side, conscious and aware, in a dimension where consciousness and awareness are all that exist.
You stand in the dark and listen to the nothing she left behind.

choices.text: The rift is sealed. Go end the war.
Tell the factions. Use the sealed rift as leverage for peace.

act4_051.title: The Synth Underground
text: The synth haven is hidden behind a false wall in sector 12 — a space carved from abandoned maintenance infrastructure, every surface insulated against Meridian’s detection scanners. Forty-seven synths live here. Some are service models, some are combat frames repurposed for civilian life, two are prototype units that were never supposed to develop personalities but did anyway.

They are scared. Meridian’s war directive includes a clause about ‘reclaiming unregistered synthetic assets’ — corporate language for hunting down free synths and wiping their consciousness cores. To Meridian, synths are property. Running away is theft — of yourself, from yourself.

A synth named Circuit leads the haven. She is an older model, her synthetic skin worn to translucency at the joints, her voice slightly desynchronized from her lip movements. She speaks with the careful precision of someone who has learned that imprecise words get synths deactivated.

"We can fight," Circuit says. "We have combat-capable frames. We have engineering units that can build fortifications in hours. We have medical synths trained in human anatomy. We are forty-seven reasons Meridian should be afraid."

"But we will not fight for a faction that will discard us when the war is over. We have seen how organics treat tools when the job is done. We need guarantees. Rights. Recognition. Or we stay behind this wall and let the organic world sort out its organic problems."

text.text: Circuit looks at you with recognition that goes deeper than facial identification. Synth to synth. A shared understanding of what it means to be alive in a body that others classify as equipment. ‘You know what I’m asking for. You know why it matters.’

choices.text: "I guarantee synth rights. Fight with me and I’ll make it happen."
"I can’t guarantee anything. But I can promise to try."
"The void rift will kill synths and organics equally. Help us stop it."

act4_052.title: Ghost Saints Offer
text: The Ghost Saints find you. They always find you. That is what they do.

Three figures materialize from shadows that should not contain them — two women and a man, dressed in urban camouflage so effective they seem to flicker in and out of visibility even standing still. The Ghost Saints are the city’s oldest faction and its most opaque. They have no declared ideology, no public leadership, no manifesto. They have contracts.

The leader — or at least the speaker, because Ghost Saints do not acknowledge leaders — is a woman whose face you cannot quite focus on. Not augmented invisibility, just a natural talent for being unmemorable. She could stand in a crowd of three and you would lose track of her.

"The Saints are prepared to act," she says. "We have assets inside Meridian’s command structure. We have operatives in every district. We can deliver Director Voss’s head on a platter, metaphorically or literally, your preference."
"The price is Highcity. After the war, the Saints control the tower district. All of it. The real estate, the infrastructure, the access to the cloud-layer broadcast network. Non-negotiable."
She tilts her head. "Or we sit this out and sell intelligence to both sides until one of them wins. We profit either way. The question is whether you want us profiting for you or against you."

choices.text: Accept. Highcity for the Saints, Voss’s head for the resistance.
Counter-offer: shared control of Highcity.
Refuse. The Saints cannot be trusted with that much power.

act4_053.title: The Drone Swarm
text: Meridian releases the swarm at noon. Two hundred combat drones, held in reserve for exactly this contingency, launched from the Highcity tower rooftops in a black cloud that blots out the anemic sun. They disperse across Midcity in a coordinated pattern designed by algorithms that do not understand mercy.

The swarm is not targeting fighters. It is targeting everyone. Meridian has reclassified the entire Lower District and Undercity population as hostile. Every heat signature, every movement, every living thing that is not transmitting a Meridian IFF code is a valid target.

The effect is immediate and devastating. Civilians who survived the tunnel floods and the street fighting are cut down in the open by precision strikes from machines that never tire, never hesitate, never feel the weight of what they are doing. A woman carrying water is hit. A man waving a white flag is hit. A dog running across an intersection is hit.
Kira’s voice on the comm is raw: "They’re killing everyone. Not fighters. Everyone. We need to take down that swarm or there won’t be anyone left to save."

text.text: The swarm uses a mesh network — each drone relays commands to its neighbors. Take down the command node and the mesh fragments. But the command node is mobile, riding on a heavy drone at the center of the formation. Getting close enough to hack it means getting close enough to be killed by it.

choices.text: Find and destroy the command drone
Get everyone underground — the drones can’t follow
This is Voss’s doing. Go straight for him.

act4_054.title: Rust’s Last Valve
text: The third drainage valve is jammed worse than the first two combined. Rust has been cranking for five minutes, the crowbar bent at a right angle from the force she has applied, and the valve has moved exactly half a turn. Not enough. The water is still rising in the south gallery.

"I need more leverage," she says through gritted teeth. Her welded armor is shedding rivets from the strain. The muscles in her arms — real muscles, not augmented, because Rust never trusted technology she could not fix with a hammer — are shaking.
A sound from above. Metal groaning. The tunnel ceiling, weakened by the flooding and the detonations, is sagging. Cracks spiderweb across the concrete. Dust falls in curtains.
"It’s going to collapse," you say.
Rust looks at the ceiling. Looks at the valve. Makes a calculation that takes less than a second.
"Get back to the gallery," she says. "Get them moving north. Now."
"Rust —"
"NOW."
You run. Behind you, you hear her scream — not in pain but in effort, the sound of a person spending everything they have on one final act. Metal screams with her. The valve turns.
The ceiling comes down.
The water drains. The gallery is saved. Three hundred people are alive because a woman in welded armor decided that a valve was worth more than her life.
You do not go back to dig. There is nothing to dig for. The collapse buried the junction under twenty meters of rock and concrete. Rust is down there, somewhere, still gripping the crowbar.
Iron Vanguard. They build things that last. Including legacies.

choices.text: Honor her sacrifice. Save everyone she died for.
Rage. Go topside and make Meridian pay.

act4_055.title: Pixel’s Message
text: The synth child’s message arrives on a frequency reserved for emergency broadcasts. It should not be possible for a child-model synth to access that frequency. But Pixel has always been more than her specifications suggested.
The message is audio only. A child’s voice, synthesized but indistinguishable from organic, speaking with the careful enunciation of someone who knows this might be the last thing they ever say.

"Hello. My name is Pixel. I am in the drainage tunnel in sector nine. There are bad people coming and I am scared. I know I am not supposed to be scared because I am a synth and synths do not feel things. But I feel things. I feel scared and I feel cold and I feel alone."

A pause. Static. Then: "The person who helped me before — you know who you are. You told me I was real. You said my feelings counted. If that was true, please come. If it was not true, then I am sorry for bothering you. I will wait here until the bad people come. It will not take long."
The broadcast ends. Your datapad shows her location: sector 9 drainage, two kilometers into the Undercity, directly in the path of Meridian’s sweep teams.
Two kilometers. Through active combat zones, flooded tunnels, and Meridian patrols. For one synth child.

text.text: She called you real. She trusted your words. In a city that classifies synths as property, your promise that her feelings mattered was the only validation she had ever received. You cannot let that promise die in a drainage tunnel.

You remember smuggling her out of the Meridian facility in Act 1. She was small enough to fit in a cargo crate. She did not cry. She held your hand through the ventilation slots and did not let go until you opened the lid in the safe house. She trusted you then. She trusts you now.

choices.text: Go to Pixel. Two kilometers through hell.
Send someone else. You are needed elsewhere.
You cannot save everyone. Focus on the war.
